definition: Cannot breathe — severe respiratory distress. North Karnataka dialect omits the standard auxiliary verb form.
Money — in crime contexts may indicate robbery or extortion.
Severe stomach pain — 'baak' is a North Karnataka intensifier implying acute distress, not mild discomfort.
A violent fight or brawl — implies active physical altercation.
Someone set fire — implies deliberate arson, North Karnataka past-tense marker '-aar'.
They have taken someone away (abduction/kidnapping). Contracted verb form typical of Dharwad-Belgaum belt.
Vehicle has crashed / accident. 'Gaadi biddaiti' is North Karnataka dialect for a road accident.
Cannot breathe — Old Mysore dialect. Vowel shift from 'usiru' to 'usru', 'bartilla' negative form.
Chest pain. 'Edi' is the Mysore-region pronunciation of 'ede' (chest).
They are beating (someone) — ongoing physical assault.
A thief has come / intruder present — active burglary in progress.
Severe dizziness / vertigo — may indicate stroke, low BP, or head injury. Mysore pronunciation of 'tale'→'tali'.
Has fever. Coastal dialect uses 'baindu' (Tulu-influenced past tense).
Flooding — water has come in. Coastal contraction 'bandhoydu' implies sudden flooding.
A murder has happened. 'Khoona' is the Urdu-influenced Hyderabad-Karnataka term for murder/homicide.
Hospital — Urdu-influenced word 'dawakhani' used across Hyderabad-Karnataka instead of standard 'aaspatre'.
Someone has been beaten — 'Saheb' is the honorific for authority figures in Hyderabad-Karnataka dialect.
Cannot breathe — Hindi. Critical respiratory distress.
They are beating (someone) — active assault in Hindi.
Fire has started — active fire in Hindi.
A thief has come — active burglary/intrusion.
Domestic gas leak — fire & explosion risk.
Street robbery targeting necklaces/chains — common urban crime term.
South Asian colloquial term for sexual harassment in public spaces.
